text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 29 September 2018 

Dr Priya Menon
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Priya Menon
Consultant Neurologist,  

Daniel Harris, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: KCH728514 NHS No. 9876543210  
12 Brookfield Road, London, SE15 3QT

Thank you for asking me to review this gentleman in our epilepsy clinic. He has a background history of migraine without aura since his late teens, which has been relatively stable and responsive to simple analgesia; however, the current clinical focus is epilepsy. He reports that his events are stereotyped with abrupt loss of awareness, bilateral tonic stiffening progressing to clonic movements, tongue biting on the lateral aspect, and post‑ictal confusion and myalgia lasting up to several hours. There is no consistent prodrome. His partner confirms episodes can occur from wakefulness and overnight from sleep.

By way of recent quantification, he experienced seven generalised tonic-clonic seizures so far in Sep, 0 in Aug, and one in Jul, occurring during both wakefulness and sleep. He denies clear precipitating factors, though he acknowledges irregular sleep patterns related to shift work in early September and missed two doses of his evening medication that month. No recent head injury, intercurrent infection, or alcohol excess was reported. He does not drive.

On history and available documentation, the semiology, bilateral onset pattern, and prior EEG demonstrating generalised spike-and-wave discharges are in keeping with a generalised epilepsy. There is no clinical suggestion of a focal onset. He has not had status epilepticus. Family history is non-contributory. Developmental history is unremarkable. Neurological examination today was normal between seizures.

Current treatment: Sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily, initiated two years ago with partial response; adherence is generally good. He previously trialled levetiracetam, which helped frequency but produced irritability and was discontinued. He uses sumatriptan occasionally for migraine, with no clear temporal association to seizures.

Plan: I have discussed with him the working diagnosis of generalised epilepsy and the need to optimise therapy. He wishes to consider options and understands safety counselling has been provided including seizure first aid, bathing supervision, and sleep regularity. I have arranged an updated EEG and drug levels, including valproate trough and LFTs. I have also requested a sleep-deprived EEG given the nocturnal occurrences and will review him with results to discuss dose optimisation versus alternative broad-spectrum therapy should seizures persist. Written information about SUDEP risk and lifestyle measures was provided.

I will keep you informed of progress and am happy to be contacted if any issues arise meanwhile.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: he experienced seven generalised tonic-clonic seizures so far in Sep, 0 in Aug, and one in Jul
He experienced seven generalised tonic-clonic seizures so far in Sep, 0 in Aug, and one in Jul, occurring during both wakefulness and sleep

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter provides explicit counts for recent months: seven generalized tonic-clonic seizures so far in September, 0 in August, and one in July. The clinic date is 29 September 2018, so September is the current month. Following the rule to select the highest frequency when multiple are present, we use the highest of these quantified periods. Within a 3-month window (July to September), total seizures = 1 (July) + 0 (August) + 7 (September) = 8 over 3 months. This is the clearest quantified frequency in the letter. The patient is not seizure free for 6 months, so seizure-free label does not apply.
